title: Distance-Based Anomaly Detection
concept: Distance-based anomaly detection borrows the same distance calculation K-Means uses to find clusters — but flags a point as anomalous when even its closest neighbors are far away.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In K-Means, how does the algorithm decide which cluster a point belongs to?

options.text: It measures the distance from the point to each centroid and assigns it to the closest one
It asks a human to label the point first
It picks a cluster at random

exercises.explain: Right! K-Means turns "which group does this belong to" into a concrete distance calculation to the nearest centroid.

cards.title: The house at the edge of town
cards.body: Every other house on the map sits shoulder to shoulder in a tight neighborhood. One house sits two miles off by itself, with nothing nearby in any direction. You don't need statistics to notice it — it's the distance to its nearest neighbors that gives it away.

cards.title: What is distance-based anomaly detection?
cards.body: Distance-based anomaly detection flags a point as anomalous when it is far from its nearest neighbors — the handful of other points most similar to it. If even the closest matches are distant, the point doesn't belong to any tight group of normal data.

cards.title: Same ruler as K-Means, different question
cards.body: K-Means (unit 31) asks: "which centroid is this point closest to?" and assigns it there no matter what. Distance-based anomaly detection asks a stricter question: "is this point close to anything?" If its nearest neighbors — centroid or not — are still far away, it gets flagged instead of assigned.

cards.title: One quiet server among thousands
cards.body: In a data center, most servers exchange traffic with a similar circle of other servers at similar volumes — their nearest neighbors are close by that measure. One server suddenly talks to machines it's never contacted before, at volumes unlike anyone nearby. Its distance to its nearest neighbors spikes, and it gets flagged.

cards.title: You don't need finished clusters first
cards.body: It's tempting to think distance-based anomaly detection requires running K-Means and picking a K value first. In reality, all it needs is a way to measure distance between points and a check against their nearest neighbors — clusters can help build intuition, but they aren't a hard requirement.

cards.title: Dense Neighborhood vs. Isolated Point
cards.body: What distance-based detection is really measuring:

left.text:
Nearest neighbors are close
Short distance to fit in
Treated as normal

right.text:
Nearest neighbors are far
Long distance to anything
Flagged as anomalous

exercises.prompt: What does distance-based anomaly detection actually measure to decide if a point is anomalous?

options.text: How far the point is from its nearest neighbors
How many total points exist in the dataset
The alphabetical order of the point's label

exercises.explain: Exactly! If a point's nearest neighbors are far away, it doesn't fit into any tight group of normal data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A point with many close, nearby neighbors would typically be flagged as an anomaly.
exercises.explain: False! A point surrounded by close neighbors fits the normal pattern — it's isolated points, far from everyone nearby, that get flagged.

exercises.sentence: A point is flagged as anomalous when even its ___ neighbors are far away.
exercises.explain: Distance-based anomaly detection checks the distance to a point's nearest neighbors — if those are still far, nothing about it fits the normal pattern.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of distance-based anomaly detection at work?

options.text: A GPS system flags one delivery truck whose route sits miles from every other truck's typical path in that city
A spreadsheet sorts trucks alphabetically by driver name
A dashboard counts the total number of trucks on the road today

exercises.explain: Correct! Measuring how far a truck's route sits from every other truck's typical path is exactly the nearest-neighbor distance idea.

exercises.prompt: A streaming platform tracks how similar each user's viewing habits are to other users, measured as distance in "taste space." Most users have several close neighbors with similar taste. One user's three nearest neighbors are still unusually far away. A teammate says, "Let's just run K-Means and see which cluster they land in." Why might distance-based anomaly detection be a better fit here?

options.text: K-Means forces every point into some cluster no matter what, while distance-based detection can flag a point as not truly belonging anywhere if its nearest neighbors are far
K-Means and distance-based anomaly detection always produce identical results, so it doesn't matter
Distance can only be measured between numbers, never between viewing habits
Anomaly detection cannot use any ideas from clustering

exercises.explain: Exactly! K-Means always assigns a point to its closest centroid, even if that centroid is still far away. Distance-based anomaly detection is built to catch exactly that case — a point with no close neighbors at all.
